name: Robusta coffee (food medium)
basis: per 100 g green (raw) coffee beans, Coffea canephora var. Robusta, dry-weight basis. NOTE: FooDB FOOD00060 is the coffee bean; roasting destroys most sucrose/free sugars and part of the chlorogenic acids and trigonelline (generating quinic/caffeic acid, niacin, melanoidins), and brewing extracts only the water-soluble fraction. Values below are for the GREEN bean, the best-characterized and least-transformed form.

components.name: sucrose
components.source: Illy & Viani, Espresso Coffee (green-coffee reviews); ScienceDirect Robusta overview
components.evidence: Green Robusta sucrose 1-5% dry matter (Robusta lower than Arabica 6-9%); midpoint ~4 g/100g. Dominant free sugar in the green bean.

components.name: D-glucose
components.source: Organic Compounds in Green Coffee Beans (ResearchGate 282595074); green-coffee composition reviews
components.evidence: Free reducing monosaccharides (glucose, fructose, mannose, arabinose, galactose, rhamnose) present at trace levels, HIGHER in Robusta than Arabica; free glucose ~0.1-0.3 g/100g.

components.name: D-fructose
components.source: Organic Compounds in Green Coffee Beans (ResearchGate 282595074)
components.evidence: Free fructose in green coffee ~0.1-0.3 g/100g; Robusta > Arabica for reducing sugars.

components.name: galactomannan + arabinogalactan (bound polysaccharide: mannose, galactose, arabinose, glucose)
components.source: Illy & Viani; green-coffee reviews (total carbohydrate ~50% dry matter)
components.evidence: Cell-wall storage polysaccharides (galactomannan ~24%, type-II arabinogalactan ~14%, cellulose) make up ~40-48% of the bean. Not free/readily fermentable but the main carbon reservoir; on hydrolysis yields mannose > galactose > arabinose > glucose.

components.name: chlorogenic acids (total; 5-CQA and isomers, feruloyl- and dicaffeoylquinic acids)
components.source: Botanical/Geographical Characterization of Green Coffee (J Agric Food Chem, doi:10.1021/jf8037117); green-coffee reviews
components.evidence: Robusta total chlorogenic acids 7-10% dry matter (higher than Arabica); midpoint ~8 g/100g. Largest organic-acid pool; esters of quinic + caffeic/ferulic acid. Individual isomers listed under keep_uncovered.

components.name: citric acid
components.source: Yeretzian et al., Acids in coffee review (Crit Rev Food Sci Nutr, doi:10.1080/10408398.2021.1957767)
components.evidence: Green coffee total aliphatic organic acids ~20-25 g/kg (2.0-2.5 g/100g), dominated by citric, quinic and malic acids; green citric ~0.5-1.0 g/100g (Robusta somewhat lower than Arabica).

components.name: quinic acid (free)
components.source: Yeretzian et al., Acids in coffee review (doi:10.1080/10408398.2021.1957767)
components.evidence: Free quinic acid ~0.3-0.8 g/100g in green coffee (rises sharply on roasting from chlorogenic-acid hydrolysis).

components.name: malic acid
components.source: Yeretzian et al. review; patent US4976983 (green Robusta malic content)
components.evidence: Green Robusta malic acid 0.12-0.36% dry weight; midpoint ~0.24 g/100g.

components.name: acetic acid
components.source: Yeretzian et al., Acids in coffee review (doi:10.1080/10408398.2021.1957767)
components.evidence: Low in green coffee (~0.03-0.1 g/100g); increases ~10-fold on roasting.

components.name: caffeine
components.source: Botanical characterization of green coffee (doi:10.1021/jf8037117); green-coffee reviews
components.evidence: Green Robusta caffeine ~1.5-2.6% dry matter (roughly double Arabica ~1%); midpoint ~2.2 g/100g. Defining Robusta metabolite and a purine N source.

components.name: trigonelline
components.source: Green-coffee reviews; Ethiopia green-coffee study (ScienceDirect S2666154324001194)
components.evidence: Green Robusta trigonelline ~0.6-0.8% dry matter (lower than Arabica ~1%); midpoint ~0.7 g/100g. Pyridine alkaloid; partial demethylation to niacin on roasting.

components.name: theobromine
components.evidence: Minor purine alkaloid, trace (<0.05 g/100g); present but not well quantified for green Robusta.

components.name: total protein
components.source: Wang et al. 2019, Coffea robusta cultivars (PMC6332462)
components.evidence: Green Robusta protein 13.4-16.4 g/100g DW (classic reviews cite ~11%); ~13 g/100g used. Amino-acid profile below is protein-derived.

components.name: L-glutamate
components.evidence: Robusta bean 1.84-2.40 g/100g DW; most abundant amino acid.

components.name: L-serine
components.evidence: 0.45-0.55 g/100g DW. (Proteinogenic L-serine; FooDB 'D-Serine' is an enantiomer artifact.)

components.name: L-cystine
components.evidence: Present in coffee protein but not quantified by Wang et al.; low. Retained from current profile with null amount.

components.name: L-tryptophan
components.evidence: Present but not quantified by Wang et al. (destroyed in acid hydrolysis); low. Retained with null amount.

components.name: total lipid (coffee oil, triacylglycerols)
components.evidence: Green Robusta oil 8.6-12.0 g/100g DW (lower than Arabica ~15%). Fatty-acid breakdown below.

components.name: linoleic acid (C18:2 n-6)
components.evidence: 920-1633 mg/100g DW; dominant fatty acid of coffee oil.

components.name: palmitic acid (C16:0)
components.evidence: 780-1300 mg/100g DW; second-most abundant.

components.name: potassium
components.evidence: Green coffee K 19,898 ug/g. Dominant mineral (~40% of total ash); ash ~4-4.8% dry matter in Robusta.

components.name: magnesium
components.evidence: Green coffee Mg 1,758 ug/g.

components.name: phosphorus (phosphate)
components.evidence: Green coffee phosphate ~0.13-0.17% dry matter; ~160 mg/100g (not measured in PMC6778417).

components.name: calcium
components.evidence: Green coffee Ca 789 ug/g.

components.name: manganese
components.source: green-coffee reviews; coffee is a recognized Mn source
components.evidence: Green coffee Mn ~1-4 mg/100g; ~2 mg/100g typical (not measured in PMC6778417).

components.name: iron
components.evidence: Green coffee Fe 60 ug/g. (Consolidates the three duplicate iron entries and 'Fe2+ mitochondria' compartment artifact in the current profile.)

components.name: sodium
components.evidence: Green coffee Na 18.6 ug/g; low.

components.name: copper
components.evidence: Green coffee Cu 14 ug/g.

components.name: zinc
components.evidence: Green coffee Zn 3.6 ug/g (some studies report up to ~1 mg/100g).

components.name: nickel
components.evidence: Green coffee Ni 0.415 ug/g; genuine trace element in coffee, retained.

components.name: selenium
components.evidence: Genuine trace element, low µg/100g range; retained with null amount (not quantified in the fetched sources).

components.name: sulfate
components.evidence: Coffee sulphate ~0.15% dry matter; sulfur source. Retained from current profile.

components.name: alpha-tocopherol (vitamin E)
components.evidence: Present in coffee oil (tens of mg per 100 g oil); genuine but not quantified here. Retained with null amount.

components.name: gamma-tocopherol
components.evidence: Dominant tocopherol in coffee oil; present, not quantified. Retained with null amount.

components.name: nicotinate (niacin, B3)
components.evidence: Low in green bean; largely generated from trigonelline demethylation on roasting. Retained with null amount.

components.name: pantothenic acid (B5)
components.evidence: Present at low levels; not quantified. Retained with null amount.

components.name: riboflavin (B2)
components.evidence: Low; retained with null amount.

components.name: thiamine (B1)
components.evidence: Low; retained with null amount.

components.name: pyridoxine (B6)
components.evidence: Low; retained with null amount.

components.name: folate (B9)
components.evidence: Low; retained with null amount.

components.name: phylloquinone (vitamin K1)
components.evidence: Plant-plausible (K1) but negligible in coffee bean. Retained with null amount.

remove.name: Cholesterol
remove.reason: Animal sterol, biologically implausible in a plant seed. Coffee lipids carry the diterpenes cafestol/kahweol and plant sterols (sitosterol/stigmasterol), not cholesterol. FooDB predicted/mis-assigned.

remove.name: Cyanocobalamin
remove.reason: Vitamin B12 is absent from plant foods (no plant B12 biosynthesis); FooDB predicted, not measured.

remove.name: Retinol
remove.reason: Preformed vitamin A (retinol) does not occur in plants; the real provitamin form is beta-carotene, which is retained. FooDB artifact.

remove.name: Iron (Fe3+)
remove.reason: Duplicate iron entry; consolidated into a single 'iron' component.

remove.name: Fe2+ mitochondria
remove.reason: Subcellular-compartment artifact (a BiGG/FooDB compartment tag), not a distinct food nutrient; consolidated into 'iron'.

remove.name: Co2+
remove.reason: Free cobalt ion; non-nutritive trace with no defined role in coffee except via B12 (which is absent). Not a growth nutrient of this food.

remove.name: Starch C12H20O10
remove.reason: Green coffee beans contain essentially no starch; their storage polysaccharides are galactomannan and type-II arabinogalactan (now represented explicitly). FooDB mis-assignment.

remove.name: Iodine c
remove.reason: Non-nutritive trace/contaminant element; not a defining nutrient of coffee.

remove.name: Fluoride
remove.reason: Non-nutritive trace/contaminant element; not a growth nutrient of the bean.

remove.name: D-Serine
remove.reason: D-enantiomer artifact; the proteinogenic form present in coffee protein is L-serine (included in the amino-acid list).

remove.name: Chromium
remove.reason: Trace/contaminant element, not quantified and not a defining coffee nutrient; drop as noise (borderline; harmless if kept).

missing_added: chlorogenic acids (total, ~8 g/100g) - the single largest organic-acid pool, absent from current profile
caffeine amount (~2.2 g/100g) - was only in uncovered names, now quantified
citric acid, quinic acid, malic acid, acetic acid - organic acids, all absent
specific free sugars with amounts (sucrose, glucose, fructose) and bound galactomannan/arabinogalactan
full quantified amino-acid profile (16 amino acids)
quantified fatty-acid profile (6 fatty acids) and total oil
quantified mineral amounts (K, Mg, Ca, Fe, Cu, Zn, Na, Ni, P, Mn) - K is dominant
keep_uncovered: Catechol / 3-methylcatechol / 4-methylcatechol / phenol / guaiacol / 4-vinylguaiacol / pyrogallol (mostly roast-derived phenolics)
sources: Wang et al. 2019, Coffea robusta cultivars, Hainan - fatty acids, amino acids, protein, lipid (PMC6332462)
Selected metals in coffee, atomic absorption - minerals (PMC6778417)
Yeretzian et al., Acids in coffee: review & meta-analysis, Crit Rev Food Sci Nutr, doi:10.1080/10408398.2021.1957767
Botanical & Geographical Characterization of Green Coffee, J Agric Food Chem, doi:10.1021/jf8037117 - caffeine, chlorogenic acids
Organic Compounds in Green Coffee Beans (ResearchGate 282595074)
Illy & Viani, Espresso Coffee: The Science of Quality - green-coffee proximate composition
US patent US4976983 - green Robusta malic acid range
notes: Dominant carbon/energy sources for microbial growth on green Robusta: the chlorogenic-acid pool (~8 g/100g, hydrolyzable to quinic + caffeic acid), sucrose (~4 g/100g), and cell-wall galactomannan/arabinogalactan (~43 g/100g, requires mannan/galactan-degrading enzymes). Free glucose/fructose are minor (~0.3 g each). Nitrogen comes from protein/amino acids (~13 g/100g, Glu/Asp-rich) plus the alkaloids caffeine (~2.2%) and trigonelline (~0.7%). Robusta is defined by high caffeine and chlorogenic acids and lower sucrose/lipid than Arabica. Minerals are K-dominated (~2 g/100g). Roasting would collapse sucrose and free amino acids and raise quinic/acetic/niacin; brewing would deliver only the water-soluble fraction (caffeine, chlorogenic acids, trigonelline, K, some sugars/acids) - flag if the intended medium is the beverage rather than the bean.
